content:
The agent-first architecture looks incredible in a keynote, but autonomous workflows just cost our team hundreds of dollars. Why senior engineers are quietly downgrading.

4 min read
Just now

--

The bill arrived on a Tuesday. $612 for roughly four hours of autonomous agent work. The feature it produced was not in production. It was not even close.
That was three weeks after our team watched the Google I/O keynote with genuine excitement. The demo was clean. The agent booked appointments, summarized documents, and chained tool calls like it had been doing this for years. We nodded along. We started building that same week.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

There is one architecture decision that changes everything about whether agents save you money or drain your budget — and most teams get it wrong in the first 48 hours. I will get to it. But first, you need to understand why the keynote is structurally incapable of showing you the real problem.
What the Keynote Never Shows You
Google’s agent stack — Gemini Pro as the reasoning core, Agent Builder for orchestration, tool-calling plumbed through Vertex — is genuinely impressive…
